app.title: Gateway Cost Explorer
app.subtitle: This is where the program cost comes from, how it is distributed over time, and why the estimate looks the way it does.
app.storyLine: Meaning first. Numbers second. Evidence third.
app.sourceNote: The Cost Explorer reads a single preprocessed JSON payload and links back to the supporting cost source files.

overview.includedSummary: The headline estimate is a program-reference current-cost view through FY2033. It includes NASA-led program cost and clearly flagged partner reference values where those help explain the full Gateway ecosystem.
overview.judgmentSummary: Annual burn plans, some subsystem splits, and reserve placement still require schedule-weighted or allocation-based judgment where no direct public burn profile exists.
overview.directSummary: 44 priced line items and multiple public award or oversight sources anchor the estimate before modeled phasing and allocation logic are applied.

majorDrivers.name: Habitation and Logistics Outpost
majorDrivers.meaning: Program-reference HALO current cost anchored to the $187,000,000 design award and $935,000,000 fabrication/integration award. Uncertainty remains elevated because of mass-reduction and rework pressure cited in GAO oversight.

majorDrivers.name: Canadarm3
majorDrivers.meaning: Program-reference value for the Canadarm3 contribution using Canadian public investment references and public design-phase award anchors. Treat as partner-reference scope, not as NASA budget authority.

majorDrivers.name: Mission Operations
majorDrivers.meaning: Crewed mission operations Artemis IV through FY2033; scaled from ISS operations at 10% intensity ratio.

majorDrivers.name: International Habitation Module
majorDrivers.meaning: Program-reference value for the ESA-led I-HAB contribution. Treat as ecosystem structural cost context supported by public partner data, not as NASA budget authority.

busiestYear.narrative: FY2025 is driven most by Habitation and Logistics Outpost and International Habitation Module. Reserve adds $65,000,000 in this year.

years.narrative: FY2019 is driven most by Power and Propulsion Element.
FY2020 is driven most by Power and Propulsion Element and Habitation and Logistics Outpost.
FY2021 is driven most by Habitation and Logistics Outpost and Canadarm3.
FY2022 is driven most by Habitation and Logistics Outpost and Canadarm3.
FY2023 is driven most by Habitation and Logistics Outpost and Canadarm3.
FY2024 is driven most by Habitation and Logistics Outpost and Canadarm3. Reserve adds $35,000,000 in this year.
FY2025 is driven most by Habitation and Logistics Outpost and International Habitation Module. Reserve adds $65,000,000 in this year.
FY2026 is driven most by Habitation and Logistics Outpost and Canadarm3. Reserve adds $125,000,000 in this year.
FY2027 is driven most by Launch Services and Canadarm3. Reserve adds $150,000,000 in this year.
FY2028 is driven most by Mission Operations and Canadarm3. Reserve adds $100,000,000 in this year.
FY2029 is driven most by Mission Operations and Launch Services. Reserve adds $50,000,000 in this year.
FY2030 is driven most by Mission Operations and Canadarm3. Reserve adds $35,000,000 in this year.
FY2031 is driven most by Mission Operations and Canadarm3.
FY2032 is driven most by Mission Operations and Program Management.
FY2033 is driven most by Mission Operations and Program Management.

categories.name: Lunar Gateway Program Total
categories.notes: Program-reference current cost through FY2033. Base value equals the refreshed annual phasing total across WBS 1.1-1.12 plus explicit program reserve detail of $560,000,000. Partner-contribution rows remain reference values rather than NASA appropriation authority.

methodology.summary: Assumptions, rollup logic, reserve treatment, and remaining judgment are all visible so the estimate can be read and challenged clearly.

cards.title: What the estimate includes
cards.summary: The rebuilt cost layer keeps the CARD as the NASA-accountable baseline, then adds clearly flagged partner reference values where the ecosystem story would otherwise be incomplete.
items: Program-reference current cost is used for structural understanding and app-facing rollups.
Partner contribution rows are labeled as reference values rather than NASA appropriated cost lines.
The headline total spans FY2020 through FY2033.

cards.title: How the estimate is built
cards.summary: Level-2 buckets reconcile to annual phasing, while level-3 rows preserve technical weighting under each parent. This keeps the estimate readable while still tying back to time-phased source data.
items: Current-cost rollups are reconciled to summed annual phasing.
Schedule timing drives the phasing profile where no public burn plan exists.
Subsystem rows remain weighted allocations unless priced evidence or direct sourcing is available.

cards.title: Reserve treatment is explicit
cards.summary: Reserve is not buried inside every subsystem. It is visible as estimation uncertainty, schedule recovery, and mass-reduction reserve so the user can see where management judgment enters the estimate.

cards.title: Where judgment still remains
cards.summary: The estimate now makes clear what is directly sourced, what is priced, what is partner reference, and what still relies on allocation or schedule judgment.
items: Public award values rarely provide a year-by-year burn plan.
Partner contribution values remain reference values rather than appropriated NASA budget lines.
Reserve annual placement is a management planning judgment informed by risk timing.

cards.title: How traceability is enforced
cards.summary: The data guide explains the evidence model: source IDs, source types, basis types, and clearly labeled judgment where a public fact does not exist.
items: Risk, milestone, and document artifacts carry clear sourcing cues.
The source register centralizes internal and public references.
The Cost Explorer surfaces evidence after meaning, not instead of meaning.

methodology.costBasisExcerpt: Times New Roman; Arial; Courier New; ;;;; Normal; Heading 1; Heading 2; Heading 3; NASA LUNAR GATEWAY PROGRAM COST BASIS OF ESTIMATE AND TRACEABILITY GUIDE Version 1.0 Updated: March 17, 2026 Supporting files: gateway_cost_estimate.csv, gateway_cost_phasing.csv, gateway_cost_estimate_detail.csv, gateway_section_b_pricing.csv, gateway_source_reference_register.csv 1. Purpose This document explains how the Gateway cost support layer was rebuilt into a presentation-grade and analytically defensible authority source. The goal is to make the cost files understandable on their own, traceable to specific source material, and usable as the authority layer beneath the web applications. The rebuild keeps the existing strong CARD in place as the primary internal scope and methodology anchor. This document does not replace the CARD.
methodology.authorityGuideExcerpt: Times New Roman; Arial; ;;;; Normal; Heading 1; Heading 2; GATEWAY DATA AUTHORITY GUIDE Version 1.0 Updated: March 17, 2026 1. Purpose This guide explains how the non-cost support files were strengthened so they function as authority-side source artifacts rather than thin app feeder tables. 2. Risk Register Standard The risk register now carries explicit source IDs, risk category, basis type, review date, confidence level, and a short traceability note.

traceability.summary: This estimate is strongest when the reader can distinguish direct priced evidence, direct public sourcing, partner reference values, and modeled allocation. The app makes those distinctions visible instead of flattening them.
